chunk_summary: This section outlines staged nutrition rehabilitation in severe malnutrition and persistent diarrhea, emphasizing cautious stabilization feeding, gradual calorie increases, and refeeding risk management.
learning_objectives: Understand the principles of staged nutrition rehabilitation in severe malnutrition.
Learn the composition and role of stabilization and catch-up diets in malnutrition management.
Identify and manage refeeding syndrome risks during nutritional rehabilitation.
Apply practical strategies for managing persistent diarrhea with energy-dense, low-lactose feeds.
key_facts: Stabilization diet provides ~75 kcal and 0.9 g protein per 100 mL, with low protein and sodium content.
Catch-up feeding targets ~100 kcal/kg/day with energy-dense feeds to support recovery.
Lactose load in animal milk should be reduced by mixing with cereals instead of diluting with water.
Refeeding risk requires monitoring electrolytes (phosphate, magnesium) and prescribing thiamine before increasing carbohydrate intake.
Daily monitoring of intake, weight, and clinical signs is essential during the transition to catch-up feeding.
reference_quote: India’s facility-based care protocols operationalize this using a stabilization diet (often termed a starter feed) that provides about 75 kcal and 0.9 g protein per 100 mL, is low in protein and sodium, and is given as small frequent feeds as soon as the child can tolerate. (Source: National Health Mission manual)
document_key_topics: Staged nutrition rehabilitation in severe malnutrition
Management of persistent diarrhea with low-lactose, energy-dense feeds
Refeeding syndrome risk and electrolyte monitoring
